metadata.title: Why Cancer Cells Are Harder to Kill With the Drug That Should Kill Them Best
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = apoptotic signal present / death pathway active / circuit working; CRIMSON #BF3339 = sabotage point / resistance mechanism / selection pressure; GOLD = editor's pen, once (B12 only)
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS (honored absolutely): no extrinsic pathway, no necroptosis/pyroptosis/ferroptosis, no venetoclax mechanism (separate card 24), no p53 transcriptional detail (PUMA/NOXA absent from narration), no IAP biochemistry beyond XIAP naming.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Pragmatist register (voices/pragmatist/VOICE.md + HAI.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the HAI outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Humanitarians AI
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: A leukemia patient has relapsed three times. Each treatment caused DNA damage. Each time, a new cell population survived.
By the third relapse, these leukemic B cells have one consistent feature: the death signal arrives but does not execute.

props.headline: By the third relapse, these leukemic B cells have one defining…

viz.prompt_seed: bone marrow biopsy microscopy slide showing dense clusters of abnormal lymphocytes, clinical pathology, editorial treatment

beats.narration_text: Chemotherapy delivers enough DNA damage to trigger apoptosis in normal cells. In these leukemic cells the same damage does not kill. The death signal arrives. Execution does not follow. Why?
The intrinsic apoptosis pathway runs through the mitochondria. DNA damage triggers a signal. That signal opens a mitochondrial membrane pore. Cytochrome c escapes. Caspases execute death.

viz.note: Intrinsic pathway spine: damage -> signal -> BCL-2 balance -> MOMP -> caspases -> death. All teal, flowing left to right. This is the intact, working circuit.

beats.narration_text: The leukemic cell has the same circuit. The signal arrives at the BCL-2 balance node. It stops there. Something is blocking execution.

viz.note: Same circuit, now with a crimson STOP block appearing at the BCL-2 balance node. Signal arrives left; nothing exits right.

beats.narration_text: Five distinct sabotage points. Each is a separate resistance mechanism. Each was selected by a prior chemotherapy exposure.
Mechanism one: BCL-2 overexpression. BCL-2 sequesters BAX — the protein that opens the mitochondrial pore. No free BAX means no pore, no cytochrome c release, no death.

viz.note: Intrinsic circuit. A crimson BLOCKER labeled 'BCL-2 HIGH' appears at the MOMP node, snapping into place. BAX held, no pore.

beats.narration_text: Mechanism two: BAX deletion. Some cells eliminate the effector protein entirely. Releasing BCL-2 does not help — there is no BAX left to open the membrane.

viz.note: Second crimson BLOCKER labeled 'BAX LOST' appears at effector position. Circuit now has two blocks.

beats.narration_text: Mechanism three: IAP upregulation. MOMP can still occur, but caspases must activate to execute death. XIAP blocks caspases directly at the execution step.

viz.note: Third crimson BLOCKER labeled 'XIAP' appears at caspase node. Three blocks on circuit now.

beats.narration_text: Mechanism four: PI3K-AKT survival signaling. AKT phosphorylates and inactivates BAD, a pro-apoptotic protein. Less BAD pressure reduces the signal load on BCL-2, making the guardian's job easier.

viz.note: Fourth crimson BLOCKER labeled 'AKT-BAD' appears at BH3-only sensor node. Four blocks.

beats.narration_text: Mechanism five: p53 mutation. p53 links DNA damage to the apoptotic signal. Mutate p53, and the damage sensor fires — but the signal never reaches the mitochondria.

viz.note: Fifth crimson BLOCKER labeled 'p53 MUT' appears at p53/sensor node. All five blocks now on circuit. The complete sabotage map.

beats.narration_text: Five independent defenses, not one. A drug that clears one leaves four intact. The death signal still cannot complete the circuit. This is why single-agent approaches fail at relapse.

viz.note: Full five-block circuit held. Pause. GOLD signal arrow attempts to cross from left; stops at first block. Emphasizes redundancy.

beats.narration_text: Selection built this wall incrementally. A leukemic clone. First chemotherapy exposure: most cells die. BCL-2-high cells survive and expand — selected.

viz.note: Many teal dots (cells); crimson selection pressure; most disappear; one BCL-2-high dot remains enlarged. Label: 'BCL-2 HIGH — SELECTED.' Illustrative.

beats.narration_text: Second exposure hits the BCL-2-high clone. Cells that have also lost BAX now survive. Third exposure: XIAP levels rise in survivors. Each treatment round selects for the next layer of resistance.

viz.note: Staircase continues. Step 2 adds 'BAX LOST'; Step 3 adds 'XIAP UP'. Each step narrows the surviving clone but hardens it. Illustrative.

beats.narration_text: The death signal arrives — it always did. Three rounds of chemotherapy selected for a five-layer resistance stack. Each layer was built by the treatment itself. CANCER BIOLOGY.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: This is part of the Cancer Biology series from Humanitarians AI.

props.tagline: AI education for humanitarian and social-impact practitioners.
